title: The Trial Continues
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: 04-27-2025 -AM- The Trial Continues - Hosea 5_1-7 - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: 1. The Leaders Are Guilty of Breaking God's Law (vv. 1-2)
subpoints: God calls out the priests and the house of the king especially for judgment
The monarchy established false worship (Jeroboam's golden calves); the priests perpetuated it for profit (Hosea 4:8)
Leaders set traps all over Israel — from Mizpah to Tabor — leading people into false and idolatrous worship
The biggest problem is not false teachers but the congregation's itching ears (2 Timothy 4:3-4)
Responsibility lies with the congregation to choose elders who represent Christ, not their own preferences
Verse 2: especially heinous leaders involved in the slaughter of children in pagan sacrifice

outline.title: 2. The People Are Guilty of Breaking God's Law (vv. 3, 5)
subpoints: God knows Ephraim and Israel — nothing is hidden from him (v. 3)
Two open sins: physical and spiritual prostitution (v. 3), and pride (v. 5)
Israel's pride testifies against her: she takes the witness stand in her own defense and condemns herself
Pride = self-reliance, acting as if autonomous; it always points us to self rather than God
Judah, though previously pictured as righteous, is also found guilty of pride

outline.title: 3. No Repentance Despite Guilt (vv. 4-7)
subpoints: Their deeds do not permit them to turn to God (v. 4, ESV): they are so entrenched in sin they cannot turn
Sin hardens the heart toward the Lord; a life-dominating sin is a dangerous flirtation with apostasy
They bring many sacrifices (v. 6) thinking outward acts will satisfy God — but their heart is not in it
The clinching accusation: they have raised pagan children (v. 7) — the ultimate evidence of their rebellion
God will withdraw himself from them — not in mercy but in indifference; the opposite of love is not caring
The new moon feast of new beginnings becomes a time of judgment's end

outline.title: Applications for Today
subpoints: Have you set traps that lead people in your sphere of influence away from the Lord?
Is pride turning you away from the Lord? Are you a witness against yourself?
Are you trusting in outward ordinances rather than faith in Christ?
Are you raising the next generation of servants of God — or pagans?

summary: A morning sermon on Hosea 5:1-7, continuing the courtroom imagery of chapter 4. God presents further evidence against Israel's guilt in three movements. First, the leaders — both priests and the royal house — are guilty: following Jeroboam's pattern, they set traps all over Israel (from Mizpah to Tabor) leading God's people into false worship and idolatry. The responsibility for appointing faithful leadership lies with the congregation. Second, the people are guilty: God knows Ephraim thoroughly (v. 3), and two sins are openly on display — prostitution and pride. Israel's own pride, when she takes the witness stand, testifies against her rather than for her. Third, despite clear guilt, there is no repentance: their sins have so hardened them they cannot turn (v. 4); they bring abundance of sacrifices (v. 6) as a substitute for heart-change; and the clinching accusation is that they have raised pagan children (v. 7). God's response is withdrawal — not wrath but indifference, which is the true opposite of love. Four application questions are drawn: about setting traps for others, about pride, about trusting outward ordinances, and about raising children in the faith.
transcript:
## Introduction: The Courtroom Continues

Hosea's prophecy is structured as a courtroom in which God is the prosecutor and creation is the judge. God goes out of his way to demonstrate that Israel deserves the judgment it receives — he is not capricious or impatient. The evidence is undeniable, the argument airtight. And every prophecy, however dark, has a little ray of sunshine, a glimpse of hope, as we will see here.

In chapter 5, God presents further evidence against Israel. Three things to see: the leaders are guilty of breaking God's law (vv. 1-2); the people are guilty of breaking God's law (vv. 3, 5); and yet, though they know they are guilty, there is no repentance (vv. 4-7).

## 1. The Leaders Are Guilty (vv. 1-2)

'Hear this, O priests! Take heed, O house of Israel! Give ear, O house of the king! For yours is the judgment, because you have been a snare to Mizpah and a net spread on Tabor. The revolters are deeply involved in slaughter, though I rebuked them all.'

All Israel is to hear the judgment of the Lord, but he especially calls out the priests and the house of the king. The monarchy started the false worship: Jeroboam, when the kingdom split, established golden calves at Dan and Bethel to keep people from going to Jerusalem to worship the true God. The priests perpetuated it for profit — the more guilty people felt, the more sin offerings they brought, and the more the priests ate (Hosea 4:8).

God describes these leaders as fowlers — people who set traps to catch birds. They set traps all over Israel: from Mizpah to Tabor. They led God's people to worship falsely and to worship false gods.

Paul tells Timothy that the real problem is not false teachers — it is that a congregation has itching ears, wanting people who will tell them what they want to hear instead of the full counsel of God (2 Timothy 4:3-4). The responsibility lies with the congregation to choose elders who are representatives of Christ to them, not representatives of their own preferences.

In verse 2, God singles out an especially heinous group of leaders as 'revolters' who are 'deeply involved in slaughter' — most scholars understand this as child sacrifice. These leaders encouraged God's people to do whatever their hearts desired. The natural man always likes to be told to do whatever he wants. But God wants what is best for his people and brings him the most glory.

## 2. The People Are Guilty (vv. 3, 5)

'I know Ephraim, and Israel is not hidden from me; for now, O Ephraim, you commit harlotry; Israel is defiled... The pride of Israel testifies to his face.'

God knows what Israel is doing because he knows the hearts of all people. We think we can deceive people — and we often can, for a long time — but there is one being we can never deceive, and that is God. There is no creature hidden from his sight; all things are naked and open to him.

Two sins are openly on display. First, physical and spiritual prostitution — we have discussed this at length already. Second, pride (v. 5). When Israel steps into the witness box to speak in her own defense, her own pride testifies against her. The one who was supposed to be a witness for the defense becomes a witness for the prosecution. And this is not secret testimony — it is an open court. Israel's pride is on full public display. Not only Israel, but Judah — previously pictured at the end of chapter 4 as the righteous ones — is also found guilty of pride.

Pride can be defined as self-reliance — acting as if you were autonomous, as if you were a law unto yourself. Pride was Satan's chief sin when he sought to take upon himself the glory that belongs to God alone. All the false gods are just variations on the same idol: self. You are either going to worship self or the true God of the Bible.

## 3. No Repentance (vv. 4-7)

'Their deeds do not permit them to turn to their God, for the spirit of harlotry is within them, and they do not know the LORD.'

The ESV translates verse 4 this way: 'Their deeds do not permit them to return to their God.' They are so committed to their sins that even though they see that they are wrong, they will not turn. Sin hardens the heart toward the Lord. If you are involved in a life-dominating sin, so in love with it that you will not turn away, you are in a very dangerous place — flirting with apostasy. Douglas Stuart says: 'People can become so habituated to sin in specific practice that reconciliation with God becomes exceedingly difficult.'

Knowing they are guilty, they try to appease God with quantity: with their flocks and their herds they go to seek the Lord (v. 6). Buckets of sacrifices. But there is no faith mixed with these sacrifices — they are just doing some outward thing. They think that if they say the right words or perform the right acts, God will get off their backs — just as in chapter 6 they will presume on his forgiveness.

The clinching accusation comes in verse 7: 'They have dealt treacherously with the LORD, for they have begotten pagan children.' This is the ultimate evidence of rebellion: they are raising their children according to the gods of the land. Do you realize the importance of raising your children as followers of the God of the Bible? Malachi 2:15 — why did God make husband and wife one? He seeks godly offspring. And a nonchalant attitude toward raising children in the faith is one of the most foolish attitudes possible, because everyone else in the world wants to influence your children. If you are not, you will be the only one not trying.

Because of all of this, God will withdraw himself — verse 7b. God is never absent in his omnipresence, but he chooses to reveal different aspects of himself. The opposite of love is not hatred — it is indifference. That is what God threatens here. Not his wrath, but his not caring. And the new moon, the joyful feast of new beginnings, becomes instead the time when judgment falls.

## Applications

Four things to take home. First: have you been setting traps that lead people in your sphere of influence away from the Lord? Think about every detail of your life — does your behavior help people around you follow God more faithfully, or does it lead them away? Second: is pride in your life a glaring witness against you? Are you at the center of your universe, or are you decreasing while Christ increases? Third: are you trusting in outward ordinances and actions, or is it faith in Christ that connects you to God? Isaiah 55:6-7 calls the wicked to seek the Lord with their hearts, not merely their rituals. Fourth: how are you doing with your children? Are you raising pagans or the next generation of servants of God? You can be here every Sunday and still raise your children as pagans if the 167 other hours of their week are not shaped by life-encompassing discipleship toward Christ.
